messages.content: Why does they couple wear crowns in an Eastern Orthodox wedding?

In an Eastern Orthodox wedding, the couple wears crowns to symbolize their roles as the new 'king and queen' of their own little kingdom - their home, family, and relationship. The crowns are also meant to represent martyrdom, as in the sacrifices the couple will make for one another and their commitment to their faith. The crowning ceremony is an important part of the wedding service and is a visible reminder of the couple's unity and their new responsibilities as husband and wife.
What is the largest thing in the world?

The largest thing in the world is the Earth's oceans, which cover about 71% of the Earth's surface and have a volume of around 1.332 billion cubic kilometers. However, if we talk about a specific object or living organism, the largest living organism is the honey fungus (Armillaria ostoyae) in Oregon, USA, which spans over 2,385 acres (965 hectares) and is considered one of the most extensive and oldest living organisms on Earth.

A "tar baby" is a concept that originates from African-American folklore, especially in the Uncle Remus stories written by Joel Chandler Harris in the late 19th century. The tar baby is a doll made of tar and turpentine, which is used to entrap the character Br'er Rabbit. When Br'er Rabbit encounters the tar baby and becomes angry that it does not respond to his greeting, he physically attacks it, only to become increasingly stuck to it due to its sticky nature.

The term "tar baby" has also come to be used metaphorically to refer to a sticky situation or problem that becomes worse the more one attempts to solve or deal with it. However, it is important to note that the term has been considered offensive and racially charged by some people, so it is advised to use caution and be aware of the context while using it.
What does "四是" mean? Can you identify the language and translate to English?

"四是" is a phrase in Chinese. The language can be either Mandarin or Cantonese, since the written characters are the same. In English, "四是" translates to "four is" or "four yes." Here's the breakdown: "四" (sì) means "four," and "是" (shì) means "is" or "yes." However, this phrase alone doesn't offer much context to determine its specific meaning or usage in a sentence.
Who is Apis in Egyptian mythology?

In Egyptian mythology, Apis (also known as Hapis or Hapi) is a sacred bull, considered to be an earthly manifestation of the god Ptah. Ptah was the creator god and god of craftsmen in Memphis, an ancient capital city of Egypt. Apis was believed to be a living symbol of the god and was highly revered in ancient Egyptian culture.

The Apis bull was identified by specific markings, such as a white diamond shape on its forehead, the image of an eagle on its back, and double hairs in its tail. Once a bull with these markings was found, it was brought to the temple in Memphis and considered sacred. There, the bull was served as an oracular deity, interpreting the future through the way it acted and moved.

When the Apis bull died, it was given an elaborate burial and replaced by another bull with the same distinctive markings. The dead bull was considered to become an intermediary between the gods and the people, similar to the role of Osiris, the god of the afterlife, in the form of a deity known as Osiris-Apis or Serapis. The mummified remains of the deceased Apis bulls were buried in the Serapeum, a large underground necropolis near Memphis.
Who is Nut in Egyptian mythology?

In Egyptian mythology, Nut (also spelled Nuit or Newet) is the goddess of the sky. She is often depicted as a woman stretching from horizon to horizon, arching her body over the earth while touching the ground with her fingers and toes. Nut was considered the mother of the stars and the sun, which traveled through her body each night and was reborn each morning.

Nut is one of the oldest deities in the Egyptian pantheon and is typically associated with the creation myths. According to one version of the myth, Nut was the daughter of the sun god Ra and the granddaughter of the primordial god of air, Shu, and the goddess of moisture, Tefnut. In these myths, Nut is married to the earth god Geb, and together they bore five children: Osiris (the god of the afterlife), Isis (the goddess of magic and healing), Set (the god of chaos and disorder), Nephthys (the goddess of night and mourning), and Horus the Elder (a sky god).

In another creation myth, Atum created Nut and Geb, and later, through separating them with his air, Shu gave birth to the cosmic elements. In both versions, Nut and Geb's separation represents the establishment of the cosmos with the sky above and the earth below.

One famous myth involving Nut tells the story of Ra forbidding her to give birth on any day of the year. In order to circumvent this restriction, Nut gambles with the moon god Khonsu, winning enough moonlight to create five additional days outside the 360-day year. Nut then gives birth to her children on these extra days, known as the "Five Days Upon the Year."
Is the goddess Nut depicted as or at least associated with cows?

Yes, the goddess Nut is sometimes associated with cows in Egyptian mythology. Nut is primarily depicted as a sky goddess arched over the earth, but in some representations, she is shown as a celestial cow, with her body covered in stars. In this form, Nut is said to be the divine nurse who gives life and nourishment to the universe. Her association with cows may also be related to the motherly, nurturing aspects of her character, as cows were symbols of fertility, nourishment, and motherhood in Egyptian culture.

Birds are not classified as reptiles; they belong to a separate group called Aves or avian dinosaurs. However, birds do have a close evolutionary relationship with reptiles, as they share a common ancestor that lived in the distant past. Birds are considered to be the descendants of a specific group of two-legged dinosaurs known as theropods, which include the famous Tyrannosaurus rex and the smaller Velociraptors.

In terms of classification, both birds and reptiles belong to a larger group called Sauropsida, which encompasses all living reptiles and birds, as well as many extinct lineages. This close evolutionary relationship is the reason why birds possess several reptile-like characteristics, such as laying hard-shelled eggs, having scales on their feet, and maintaining a single reproductive and excretory opening called the cloaca.
